caveats: a: No public nuclear-specific wage-weighted task study.
a: The three-firm estimate makes representative sampling difficult.
rho: Bounded judgment rather than observed adoption.
rho: Autonomous reactor control and safety disposition are excluded.
e: Utility margin bridge is poorly suited to nuclear plants with large fixed obligations.
e: Establishments, licenses, owners, and transferable operating firms are not equivalent.
s5: Economy-wide owner-age proxy is especially weak.
s5: Asset transfers outside LMM must be excluded.
q: Commercial structure varies materially by plant and region.
q: No public contract sample measures AI-benefit retention.
d5: Near-term forecast does not ensure five-year growth.
d5: Advanced reactors may not enter the current service basket or LMM ownership.
o: Operator consolidation may change ownership, not eliminate operator need.
o: This is separate from labor efficiency within a plant.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring regulated, merchant, capacity, or long-term contracted revenue from licensed baseload nuclear generation, with extensive operations, maintenance, security, radiation-protection, fuel, and compliance obligations.
lens.excluded_activities: Vertically integrated captive units, government and research reactors, passive financing vehicles without operations, decommissioning-only entities, shells, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent LMM operators of commercially licensed nuclear generation facilities that repeatedly provide electricity and capacity to external transmission systems, distribution utilities, markets, or contracted customers.

a.bridge: BLS power-plant occupation evidence covers all generation technologies rather than nuclear, while NRC sources establish individually licensed operators, examinations, simulator tests, inspection, and safety oversight. I map document retrieval, work planning, predictive maintenance, outage scheduling, procedure cross-checking, compliance drafting, inventory, and data analysis as exposed; licensed control, field operations, maintenance execution, radiation protection, chemistry, security, emergency response, and accountable safety decisions are less substitutable. The broad proxy likely overstates substitution because nuclear work is more procedure- and review-constrained.
caveats: No public nuclear-specific wage-weighted task study.
The three-firm estimate makes representative sampling difficult.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task inventories at several nuclear stations, including current digital-work-management and analytics adoption and NRC-approved staffing constraints, would materially revise the estimate.
a.rationale: The information burden is large, but safety, licensing, and defense-in-depth requirements keep most labor accountable and physically tied to the facility.

d5.bridge: EIA forecasts nuclear's national generation share holding at 18% through 2027 and a January 2026 forecast of 2%, or 15 BkWh, nuclear-generation growth over 2025; DOE also identifies uprates and restarts. These are two-year national indicators and policy-backed projects, not five-year constant-quality demand for eligible LMM operators. I extend them to modest five-year growth with downside for outages, closures, and licensing delays.
caveats: Near-term forecast does not ensure five-year growth.
Advanced reactors may not enter the current service basket or LMM ownership.
d5.change_evidence: EIA reactor-level generation and capacity projections, NRC license extensions, uprate applications, restart milestones, ISO capacity awards, and target outage plans would materially revise the range.
d5.rationale: Rising electricity and data-center load, uprates, reactor restarts, life extension, and reliability needs support stable-to-growing nuclear output, while unplanned outages, closure economics, and regulatory delays remain risks.

caveats: Utility margin bridge is poorly suited to nuclear plants with large fixed obligations.
Establishments, licenses, owners, and transferable operating firms are not equivalent.
e.change_evidence: Review all three candidates using NRC operating licenses, EIA ownership, staffing, market participation, rate filings, decommissioning status, debt, and audited EBITDA.
e.rationale: The injected universe is only three and commercial reactors typically sit inside large utility or generation portfolios, while standalone entities may be passive owners or decommissioning structures. A true independent LMM licensed operator with transferable staff, security, financing, and recurring external sales is rare.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 221113 ($6,188,459,120) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 221113 ($33,078,512,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3492 (margins.json[22]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Utility (General) EBITDA/Sales 34.92%); revenue/employee $861,958 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 221113); class <5: 8 firms, avg revenue/firm $162,750 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $56,832 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,575,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,692,790 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 21 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,547,485,143 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $540,381,812 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 3

caveats: Operator consolidation may change ownership, not eliminate operator need.
This is separate from labor efficiency within a plant.
o.change_evidence: NRC approval of materially reduced staffing, remote multi-unit control, or autonomous commercial reactor operation would move the estimate.
o.rationale: Any retained nuclear output requires a licensed facility operator with qualified staff, security, radiation protection, procedures, and NRC oversight. Software can assist workflows but cannot replace the legal and operational entity responsible for reactor safety.

caveats: Commercial structure varies materially by plant and region.
No public contract sample measures AI-benefit retention.
q.change_evidence: Rate-case treatment, PPAs, capacity contracts, market offers, outage incentive mechanisms, and operating-company sharing provisions would directly estimate benefit pass-through.
q.rationale: Regulated cost-of-service frameworks, rate reviews, and long-term power contracts can return savings to customers, while competitive markets eventually reflect lower costs in offers. Fixed-price arrangements, capacity revenue, reliability value, and superior outage execution allow partial retention.

caveats: Bounded judgment rather than observed adoption.
Autonomous reactor control and safety disposition are excluded.
rho.change_evidence: NRC-observed pilots measuring labor hours, human review, procedure errors, work-order quality, outage duration, and reliability without reducing licensed staffing would move the range.
rho.rationale: Controlled assistance for document search, planning, predictive maintenance, procedure comparison, outage analytics, and compliance drafting is feasible within five years. Nuclear cyber controls, validated procedures, configuration management, operator licensing, staffing requirements, regulator acceptance, and catastrophic error costs sharply constrain implementation.

s5.bridge: Federal Reserve owner-age evidence covers small businesses generally rather than nuclear license transfers. I apply a very large discount for the three-firm pool, large-corporate ownership, NRC approval, financing assurance, decommissioning liabilities, antitrust and public review, PPA or rate approvals, and internal restructurings. Nuclear plants can change owners, but such transactions usually involve large strategic assets outside the LMM lens.
caveats: Economy-wide owner-age proxy is especially weak.
Asset transfers outside LMM must be excluded.
s5.change_evidence: NRC license-transfer dockets, EIA ownership histories, FERC Section 203 orders, state commission approvals, and S&P Global transactions since 2000 would establish a denominator-based rate.
s5.rationale: Qualifying LMM control transfers are exceptional because ownership changes require regulator scrutiny and the actionable independent population is nearly absent.

principal_driver: Long-lived licensed plants provide high-reliability baseload output into growing electricity demand.
principal_weakness: The transferable LMM firm population is nearly nonexistent and safety rules constrain labor substitution.

sources.supports: NRC licenses all individuals who operate or supervise controls at commercially owned power reactors and oversees initial and requalification examinations.
sources.title: NRC Operator Licensing

sources.supports: NRC describes written examinations, plant walkthroughs, simulator performance demonstrations, and facility-certified training and experience.
sources.title: NRC Licensing Process for Reactor Operators

sources.supports: NRC inspects organizational structure, operator qualifications, design, maintenance, fuel handling, environmental programs, and radiation protection at licensed plants.
sources.title: NRC Inspection and Safety Oversight

sources.supports: NRC maintains active license-transfer reviews and explains that plant personnel, procedures, and policies typically are not themselves part of a transfer.
sources.title: NRC License Transfers and Mergers

sources.supports: EIA forecasts nuclear at 18% of U.S. generation in 2025, 2026, and 2027 and electricity demand rising 1% in 2026 and 3% in 2027.
sources.title: EIA Forecasts Strong Electricity-Demand Growth Through 2027

sources.supports: Federal Reserve owner-age evidence used only as a broad succession proxy.
sources.title: 2024 Chartbook on Firms by Age of Primary Owner

sources.supports: DOE describes initiatives to increase output through uprates, restart dormant facilities, and complete stalled projects amid industrial and data-center load growth.
sources.title: The Nation's Nuclear Reactor Fleet Is on the Rise

title: Nuclear Electric Power Generation
